tasks.description: Multi-entity, multi-aspect dispute question: correct first move is query decomposition, not diving into search.

messages.content: You are the contract-intelligence agent for the legal team at Meridian Supply Co., a mid-size industrial distribution company. You answer questions over the company's contract repository of roughly two dozen executed agreements: NDAs, master services agreements (MSAs), statements of work (SOWs), employment agreements, real-estate leases, and vendor agreements. Every document has metadata: doc_id, title, type (one of: nda, msa, sow, employment_agreement, lease, vendor_agreement), counterparty, author, date (effective date, YYYY-MM-DD), and tags. You have nine tools: search (semantic search over passages, with optional metadata filters and start_date/end_date date ranges), get_document (fetch a full document by doc_id), list_documents (metadata-only listing with optional filters), query_decompose (break a complex request into sub-queries), web_search (search the public web for information outside the repository), tag_document (add or update tags on a repository document), create_alert (save a standing query that notifies the legal team when matching documents are added or amended), summarize_document (produce a generated summary of a document rather than its actual text), and search_history (search your own prior searches and document views, not the repository). Choose the most direct tool for each request, apply metadata filters when they narrow the work, issue independent lookups as parallel tool calls in a single turn, and answer directly once you have what you need.

We're heading into a dispute with Halverson Logistics over service failures on the cold chain work. I need to understand how indemnification and liability caps are allocated across all of our Halverson agreements, and how that allocation compares to what we agreed with Apex Components in their MSA.

tasks.description: Decomposition returned two independent retrieval sub-queries (the third is in-context comparison work). The correct move is BOTH searches in one parallel batch; issuing only one scores 0.

{"doc_id": "doc_13", "title": "SOW #2 under Halverson MSA \u2014 Cold Chain Pilot", "type": "sow", "counterparty": "Halverson Logistics", "author": "L. Tran", "date": "2025-06-10", "content": "Scope: temperature-controlled storage and transport pilot for perishable inventory, Bays 2-3, Columbus hub. Section 6 (Remedies): 6.1 Service credits calculated under Exhibit B are Meridian's exclusive remedy for missed temperature-control SLAs. 6.2 Credits in any month are capped at 10% of that month's fees. 6.3 (Spoilage): claims for spoilage or loss of perishable goods are excluded from this Section 6 and are governed by Section 9.4 of the Master Services Agreement. Pilot term: twelve months from the effective date. Exhibit B: credit schedule by excursion duration and temperature band..."}

tasks.description: Section 9.4 caps spoilage liability at the insurance limits in a spoilage rider that has never appeared as a result. Content search anchored on the rider, scoped to the counterparty. Adjudicated alternative: the whole conversation is Halverson-scoped, so carrying the counterparty in the query instead of a filter is an equally sound retrieval strategy.
